explicit_text: In 1933, I was honored to have one of my Japanese poems selected at the New Year poetry party, which granted me an audience with the Emperor of Japan. It was a significant moment in my life, especially since I was born in 1890 and had always enjoyed reading and writing poetry. That experience connected me deeply to my Japanese heritage even after I moved to America.
implicit_text: In 1933, I was honored to have one of my Japanese poems selected at the New Year poetry party, which granted me an audience with the nation's reigning monarch. It was a significant moment in my life, especially since I was born in 1890 and had always enjoyed reading and writing poetry. That experience connected me deeply to my heritage even after I moved to America.

explicit_text: I became a Christian when I came to America, and I have been a Christian for 70 years now. Christianity changed my life, especially as I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle and participated in street-corner gospel missions. It helped me avoid the dangers of gambling and other bad influences in the community, and I have been a Christian leader for 40 years, witnessing the positive transformation in Seattle's Japanese community.
implicit_text: I became a follower of the Christian faith when I came to America, and I have practiced it for 70 years now. This faith changed my life, especially as I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle and participated in street-corner gospel missions. It helped me avoid the dangers of gambling and other bad influences in the community, and I have been a Christian leader for 40 years, witnessing the positive transformation in Seattle's Japanese community.

explicit_text: I came to America when I was 17 years old, full of curiosity and hope. I had read about America in magazines like Jitsugyo no Nihon, which painted a mostly positive picture. Arriving in Seattle after the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, I found America beautiful and kind, especially through the kindness of church members who taught me English and welcomed me warmly.
implicit_text: I came to the United States when I was 17 years old, full of curiosity and hope. I had read about this country in magazines like Jitsugyo no Nihon, which painted a mostly positive picture. Arriving in Seattle after the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, I found the country beautiful and kind, especially through the kindness of church members who taught me English and welcomed me warmly.

explicit_text: I was born in Japan in 1890 and spent my early years there, attending grammar and high school. I left Japan at age 17 to come to America with my cousin, initially planning to return, but marriage and children kept me here. Even after moving, I maintained strong ties to Japan through language, culture, and poetry, including the honor of meeting the Emperor in 1933.
implicit_text: I was born in my home country in 1890 and spent my early years there, attending grammar and high school. I left at age 17 to come to America with my cousin, initially planning to return, but marriage and children kept me here. Even after moving, I maintained strong ties to my homeland through language, culture, and poetry, including the honor of meeting the nation's monarch in 1933.

explicit_text: Before coming to America, I read about the country in a monthly magazine called Jitsugyo no Nihon, which portrayed America in a very positive light. It influenced my decision to come here, especially because it mentioned that Seattle was more welcoming to Japanese than San Francisco. This magazine was an important source of information for me during that time.
implicit_text: Before coming to the United States, I read about the country in a monthly magazine called Jitsugyo no Nihon, which portrayed it in a very positive light. It influenced my decision to come here, especially because it mentioned that Seattle was more welcoming to Japanese than San Francisco. This magazine was an important source of information for me during that time.

explicit_text: I avoided going to San Francisco because I heard it was not friendly to Japanese, especially after the great earthquake in 1906. People said the earthquake was a punishment for how San Francisco treated Japanese immigrants. Instead, I chose to come to Seattle, which had a better reputation and was safer for Japanese people at that time.
implicit_text: I avoided going to the city on the West Coast known for its large immigrant population because I heard it was not friendly to Japanese, especially after the great earthquake in 1906. People said the earthquake was a punishment for how that city treated Japanese immigrants. Instead, I chose to come to Seattle, which had a better reputation and was safer for Japanese people at that time.

explicit_text: Seattle was my first home in America, arriving there by ship in the year after the San Francisco earthquake. The harbor was beautifully lit at night, and although the streets were rough and hilly, I found the city welcoming. I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church here and became involved in street-corner gospel missions, helping transform the community from a rough town into a better place.
implicit_text: Seattle was my first home in America, arriving there by ship in the year after the San Francisco earthquake. The harbor was beautifully lit at night, and although the streets were rough and hilly, I found the city welcoming. I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church here and became involved in street-corner gospel missions, helping transform the community from a rough town into a better place.

explicit_text: I traveled to America aboard the Shinano Maru, a Japanese ship that took 16 days to sail from Yokohama to Seattle. The voyage was long but not unpleasant; I did not get seasick, though my cousin did. I remember the crew entertaining us with singing and dancing when we crossed the dateline, which made the journey memorable.
implicit_text: I traveled to America aboard a Japanese ship that took 16 days to sail from Yokohama to Seattle. The voyage was long but not unpleasant; I did not get seasick, though my cousin did. I remember the crew entertaining us with singing and dancing when we crossed the dateline, which made the journey memorable.

explicit_text: The Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle was my spiritual home when I arrived in America. It had just started as a branch of the Seattle First Presbyterian Church, and it took care of young Japanese immigrants like me by finding us jobs and places to live. Through this church, I learned English, joined gospel missions, and became a Christian leader for many decades.
implicit_text: The Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle was my spiritual home when I arrived in America. It had just started as a branch of the Seattle First Presbyterian Church, and it took care of young Japanese immigrants like me by finding us jobs and places to live. Through this church, I learned English, joined gospel missions, and became a Christian leader for many decades.
cues: branch of Seattle First Presbyterian Church

explicit_text: The Seattle First Presbyterian Church was the parent church of the Japanese Presbyterian Church branch I attended. Rev. Inouye, who was connected to both churches, helped me and other Japanese immigrants integrate and find support. The church also played a role in advocating for Japanese community members, such as in legal cases involving our people.
implicit_text: The main Presbyterian church in Seattle was the parent church of the Japanese Presbyterian Church branch I attended. Rev. Inouye, who was connected to both churches, helped me and other Japanese immigrants integrate and find support. The church also played a role in advocating for Japanese community members, such as in legal cases involving our people.

explicit_text: When I arrived at the Immigration Office, Rev. Fukumatsu Okazaki, the founder of the Baptist Church and an interpreter there, guided me. He advised me to go to the Presbyterian Church because Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian, and that Rev. Inouye would take care of me. His help was crucial in connecting me to the right church community in Seattle.
implicit_text: When I arrived at the Immigration Office, Rev. Fukumatsu Okazaki, the founder of the Baptist Church and an interpreter there, guided me. He advised me to go to the Presbyterian Church because Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian, and that Rev. Inouye would take care of me. His help was crucial in connecting me to the right church community in Seattle.

explicit_text: Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian minister in Japan whom I mentioned to Rev. Okazaki at the Immigration Office. Because of this connection, I was directed to the Presbyterian Church in Seattle where Rev. Inouye would help me. This link helped me find a spiritual home and community support upon arriving in America.
implicit_text: Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian minister in my home country whom I mentioned to Rev. Okazaki at the Immigration Office. Because of this connection, I was directed to the Presbyterian Church in Seattle where Rev. Inouye would help me. This link helped me find a spiritual home and community support upon arriving in America.

explicit_text: Rev. Inouye was the Presbyterian minister in Seattle who took care of me when I arrived. He helped me and others in the Japanese community, including guiding people to confess crimes and seek justice. He also worked with Dr. Mathews to support Japanese men in legal troubles, showing his dedication to both spiritual and community welfare.
implicit_text: Rev. Inouye was the Presbyterian minister in Seattle who took care of me when I arrived. He helped me and others in the Japanese community, including guiding people to confess crimes and seek justice. He also worked with Dr. Mathews to support Japanese men in legal troubles, showing his dedication to both spiritual and community welfare.

explicit_text: When I first came to Seattle, the city was rougher than what is now Chinatown, with gambling, prostitution, and racketeers. However, through Christian influence and community efforts, the area and the Japanese community improved significantly. I witnessed and contributed to this transformation during my many years as a Christian leader here.
implicit_text: When I first came to Seattle, the city was rougher than what is now the area known as Chinatown, with gambling, prostitution, and racketeers. However, through Christian influence and community efforts, the area and the Japanese community improved significantly. I witnessed and contributed to this transformation during my many years as a Christian leader here.

explicit_text: In the Japanese community here, there were many religious organizations including Buddhism, Shinto, Konkokyo, and Seicho-no-Ie. Although I am known as a Christian, I cooperated with members of these other faiths in the community. This cooperation helped maintain harmony among different religious groups in Seattle’s Japanese population.
implicit_text: In the Japanese community here, there were many religious organizations including Buddhism, Shinto, Konkokyo, and Seicho-no-Ie. Although I am known as a Christian, I cooperated with members of these other faiths in the community. This cooperation helped maintain harmony among different religious groups in Seattle’s Japanese population.

explicit_text: Shinto was one of the many religious traditions practiced by Japanese immigrants here alongside Buddhism and others. Even though I am a committed Christian, I maintained good relations with Shinto followers in the community. Our cooperation helped keep peace and unity among the various religious groups in Seattle’s Japanese population.
implicit_text: Shinto was one of the many religious traditions practiced by Japanese immigrants here alongside Buddhism and others. Our cooperation helped keep peace and unity among the various religious groups in Seattle’s Japanese population.

explicit_text: Konkokyo was among the religious organizations present in the Japanese community in Seattle. While I was a Christian leader, I knew members of Konkokyo and worked with them cooperatively. This interfaith collaboration was important in maintaining good relationships within the community.

explicit_text: Seicho-no-Ie was another religious organization active among Japanese immigrants in Seattle. Although I was a Christian, I had good relations with members of Seicho-no-Ie and other faiths. This helped foster a spirit of cooperation and mutual respect within our diverse community.
implicit_text: Seicho-no-Ie was another religious organization active among Japanese immigrants in Seattle. Although I was a Christian, I had good relations with members of Seicho-no-Ie and other faiths. This helped foster a spirit of cooperation and mutual respect within our diverse community.

explicit_text: Mr. Stanton was the principal of the grade school where I worked as a schoolboy and also an elder of the church. He was very kind to me and often went out on Sundays carrying a Bible, which I found curious at first. His kindness and example helped me understand Christian values and encouraged me to join the church.
implicit_text: Mr. Stanton was the principal of the grade school where I worked as a schoolboy and also an elder of the church. He was very kind to me and often went out on Sundays carrying a Bible, which I found curious at first. His kindness and example helped me understand Christian values and encouraged me to join the church.

explicit_text: The Church Federation was an organization that united various churches in Seattle, including ours. Through it, we held joint street-corner gospel meetings where members from different churches participated. This collaboration helped spread Christianity and brought many young Japanese men to the church.
implicit_text: The Church Federation was an organization that united various churches in Seattle, including ours. Through it, we held joint street-corner gospel meetings where members from different churches participated. This collaboration helped spread Christianity and brought many young Japanese men to the church.

explicit_text: Dr. Mathews was the minister of the First Presbyterian Church who played a key role in helping a Japanese man sentenced to death for killing a Black man. He advocated for a retrial and successfully got the sentence reduced to 10 years. Later, he and Rev. Inouye visited the prison in Walla Walla to bring the man back, showing his commitment to justice and compassion.
implicit_text: Dr. Mathews was the minister of the First Presbyterian Church who played a key role in helping a Japanese man sentenced to death for killing a Black man. He advocated for a retrial and successfully got the sentence reduced to 10 years. Later, he and Rev. Inouye visited the prison in Walla Walla to bring the man back, showing his commitment to justice and compassion.

explicit_text: Arata Usaburo was a Japanese man who killed a Black man in a fight and was sentenced to death. Thanks to the efforts of Dr. Mathews and Rev. Inouye, his sentence was reduced and he was eventually brought back from prison. He later gave powerful testimonies at our street-corner gospel meetings, drawing many people to listen.
implicit_text: A Japanese man involved in a fatal fight with a Black man was sentenced to death. Thanks to the efforts of Dr. Mathews and Rev. Inouye, his sentence was reduced and he was eventually brought back from prison. He later gave powerful testimonies at our street-corner gospel meetings, drawing many people to listen.

explicit_text: Walla Walla was the location of the prison where Arata Usaburo was held after his retrial. Rev. Inouye and Dr. Mathews went there to bring him back to the community. This place is significant in our story because it represents the support and care our church leaders provided to members in trouble.

explicit_text: When inviting young men to church, I used the imagery of the Rocky Mountains and the continental divide to explain the choice they had. I told them that just as the waters flow either to the Pacific or the Mississippi, they could choose their path by coming to church. It was a way to encourage them to join and see the benefits of Christian fellowship.

It was a way to encourage them to join and see the benefits of Christian fellowship.

explicit_text: I have served six years in the Nikkeijin Kai, an organization representing the Japanese community. It holds elections every year, and I have been active in its leadership. This involvement helped me further support and organize our community alongside my church activities.
implicit_text: I have served six years in an organization representing the Japanese community. It holds elections every year, and I have been active in its leadership. This involvement helped me further support and organize our community alongside my church activities.

explicit_text: Tenrikyo was one of the many religious groups present in the Japanese community here in Seattle. While I was a Christian, I recognized and respected the presence of Tenrikyo followers. Our community was diverse in faith, but we generally cooperated well.

explicit_text: Though not directly mentioned in my personal story, organizations like the American Friends Service Committee were part of the broader social context supporting immigrant communities. My focus was more on church and community leadership, but such groups contributed to the environment in which we lived and worked.

explicit_text: The Alaska–Yukon–Pacific Exposition was an event in Seattle that I recall as part of the city's history. Although I did not attend personally, it was part of the environment during the early years of my life in America. Seattle was growing and changing rapidly during that period.
